title: The Body and the Blood
description: Alice, Sweet Alice isn't a theological statement. Not solely. It doesn't posit lazy anti-Catholic bloviations or make the faithful into fools; it uses the imagery, the fables, the metaphors as inspiration for making a smartly, sickly compelling narrative of wickedness and the tragedy of tainted innocence.
hook: A Catholic horror film that uses religious imagery not to condemn faith but to map how wickedness infects the innocent—aesthetics as moral language.

title: My Brain Told Me Food Was the Enemy of Love
description:
“Reader, I Gained Weight”: On Eating Disorders and Romance by Katherine J. Chen I had forgotten the taste of bread. Of salmon and chicken. Of chocolate and figs. My tongue held the memory of these foods, of a cube of Kobe beef so tender that it felt a profanity to chew, of a spoonful of […]

The post My Brain Told Me Food Was the Enemy of Love appeared first on
hook: She starved herself believing food was incompatible with being lovable. A memoir that makes eating disorders legible as distorted logic, not vanity.

title: Idiots: On Munch and von Trier
description: “Not direct access to reality, of course, but a different kind of access.”
hook: Why Munch and von Trier both traffic in idiocy as a route to truth—when formal chaos becomes the only honest response to human damage.

title: The Children by Melissa Albert review – intriguing fairytale of creativity’s dangers
description:
In her first novel for adults, the YA author explores the dark side of writers who fictionalise their children’s lives

Children’s writers are sometimes cruel, and often damaged. And, as AS Byatt put it crisply when talking about her 2009 novel The Children’s Book: “Writing children’s books isn’t good for the writer’s own children.” Think of Christopher Milne, raging at having been Christopher Robin; Vivian Burnett, dragging Little Lord Fauntleroy behind him; Alastair Grahame, lying down
hook: What happens when writers fictionalize their own children's lives: Melissa Albert's debut explores the cruelty hiding inside creative ambition and parental damage.

title: In the Reality Lab
description: The human hand has twenty-nine bones and twenty-nine major joints. It contains over one hundred ligaments, connected to thirty-four muscles in the palm alone, each one responsible for the minute negotiations that allow us to tie our shoes, thread a needle, lift a glass, or juggle. Thirty arteries pump blood alongside them. Forty-eight nerves—running from […]
hook: Inside the science labs building artificial bodies—what hands reveal about how we've outsourced our understanding of touch and control to machines.
